_metadata.description: Maps clinical pathway names (from existing HTML app) to precise cross-sectional zones at each spinal cord anatomical level. This is the key data structure that enhances localization resolution beyond the original 29-region binary system.
_metadata.region: Spinal Cord (C3 to T5, plus cervicomedullary junction)

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Fasciculus cuneatus / Medial lemniscus (upper limb component)

caudal-medulla.vascular_territory: anterior spinal artery / vertebral artery branches

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: At spinal cord levels, this pathway is in the dorsal columns (posterior funiculus). After synapsing in nucleus cuneatus, it crosses as internal arcuate fibers and becomes the medial lemniscus in the medulla. The vascular territory shifts from posterior spinal artery (cord) to ASA territory (medulla).

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Fasciculus gracilis / Medial lemniscus (lower limb component)

caudal-medulla.vascular_territory: anterior spinal artery / vertebral artery branches

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Fasciculus gracilis is present at ALL spinal cord levels (unlike cuneatus which starts at T6). Always in medial position of posterior funiculus.

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Lateral spinothalamic tract (upper limb somatotopy) + Anterior spinothalamic tract

SC-cervical.zone: anterolateral funiculus; upper limb fibers are MEDIAL (closer to gray matter)

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: In the anterolateral system, there is a laminar somatotopy: sacral fibers most superficial/lateral, cervical fibers most deep/medial. So hand>leg pattern means the more medial fibers are affected (deeper lesion or central cord syndrome).

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Lateral spinothalamic tract (lower limb somatotopy) + Anterior spinothalamic tract

SC-cervical.zone: anterolateral funiculus; lower limb fibers are LATERAL (more superficial)

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Leg fibers are most superficial/lateral in the anterolateral system at cervical levels. A superficial/lateral lesion preferentially affects leg pain/temperature.

SC-cervical.zone: lateral funiculus, posterior part; arm fibers are MEDIAL (closer to gray matter)

SC-cervicomedullary junction (pyramidal.zone: ventral-medial (pyramid), then crossing to contralateral lateral funiculus; arm fibers decussate MORE ROSTRALLY

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: In the lateral CST at cervical levels, arm fibers are medial and leg fibers are lateral. Central cord syndrome preferentially affects arm fibers. At the pyramidal decussation, arm fibers cross more rostrally than leg fibers.

SC-cervical.zone: lateral funiculus, posterior part; leg fibers are LATERAL (more superficial)
SC-cervical.vascular_territory: anterior spinal artery (lateral branches) / posterior spinal artery overlap

SC-cervicomedullary junction (pyramidal.zone: ventral-medial (pyramid); leg fibers decussate MORE CAUDALLY

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Leg fibers are lateral/superficial in the lateral CST at cervical levels. A lesion at the decussation that affects the caudal part preferentially impairs leg function.

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Posterior spinocerebellar tract + Anterior spinocerebellar tract

SC-all-levels.zone: lateral funiculus, SURFACE; PSCT dorsal, ASCT ventral
SC-all-levels.quadrant: lateral-dorsal (PSCT) and lateral-ventral (ASCT), both on surface

SC-cervicomedullary junction.zone: lateral surface, forming inferior cerebellar peduncle (PSCT) or continuing to pons (ASCT)

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Both spinocerebellar tracts travel on the surface of the lateral funiculus. PSCT enters cerebellum via inferior cerebellar peduncle (at medulla level). ASCT continues up to enter via superior cerebellar peduncle (at pons level). Clarke's nucleus (origin of PSCT) is at T1-L2.

SC-cervical.zone: lateral funiculus, immediately ANTERIOR to lateral CST

SC-thoracic.zone: lateral funiculus (very small at this level)

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: In humans, the rubrospinal tract is small and primarily present at cervical levels. It facilitates fine distal movements and serves as a partial backup for the corticospinal tract.

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Descending hypothalamospinal (sympathetic) pathway + Intermediolateral cell column

SC-cervical (descending fibers).zone: lateral funiculus / lateral reticular formation (descending sympathetic fibers from hypothalamus)

SC-thoracic T1-T2 (ciliospinal center).zone: intermediolateral cell column (lateral horn) at T1-T2
SC-thoracic T1-T2 (ciliospinal center).special: Ciliospinal center of Budge (C8-T2). Preganglionic sympathetic neurons for eye (pupil dilation, lid elevation, facial sweating).

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Damage to the descending sympathetic pathway at any level from hypothalamus to T1-T2 causes ipsilateral Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis). At spinal cord levels, the fibers are in the lateral funiculus. At medullary levels, they are in the lateral tegmentum (Wallenberg syndrome).

SC-all-levels.zone: anterior funiculus (pontine/medial reticulospinal) + anterolateral funiculus (medullary/lateral reticulospinal)

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Reticulospinal tracts are scattered in the anterior and anterolateral funiculi. The pontine reticulospinal is more medial; medullary reticulospinal is more lateral. They influence posture, locomotion, and autonomic function.

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus (pars interpolaris and pars caudalis)

SC-upper-cervical (C1-C3).zone: lateral surface, replacing dorsolateral tract position

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: The spinal trigeminal tract extends from pons down to C2-C3 level. At spinal cord levels, it occupies the position of Lissauer's tract on the lateral surface. Pars caudalis handles pain/temperature of face. Important in lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome.

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Corticobulbar fibers to spinal accessory nucleus

SC-upper-cervical (C1-C5).zone: the target nucleus (spinal accessory nucleus) is in the lateral ventral horn at C1-C5/C6

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: The spinal accessory nucleus extends from C1 to C5-C6. It receives bilateral corticobulbar input (for SCM) and contralateral input (for trapezius). The corticobulbar fibers reach this nucleus by traveling through the medullary pyramid and lateral funiculus.

pathway_zone_mappings.underlying_tract: Solitary tract and nucleus tractus solitarius

caudal-medulla.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, near floor of 4th ventricle

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: The solitary tract is present at the caudal medulla level (p-05). Rostral part handles taste (CN VII, IX). Caudal part handles visceral sensation (CN IX, X). Located in dorsolateral medulla, PICA territory.

caudal-medulla.zone: hypoglossal nucleus is in the dorsal medulla, paramedian, floor of 4th ventricle

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: CN XII nucleus is a medial medulla structure. Medial medullary syndrome affects the pyramid + medial lemniscus + CN XII root. Corticobulbar input is primarily contralateral.

caudal-medulla.zone: dorsolateral medulla, lateral to 4th ventricle floor

pathway_zone_mappings.notes: Vestibular nuclei begin at the cervicomedullary junction and extend through medulla and pons. Located in lateral medulla (PICA territory). Damage causes vertigo, nystagmus, and vomiting.
